summary: McKenzie describes a recurring pattern of customers not receiving their automated registration key emails after purchase, leading to support issues and even a double purchase. After three incidents in quick succession — a basic support request, a bounced email from a school's spam filter, and a customer who repurchased thinking their first transaction failed — he decides to display registration keys directly on the website rather than relying solely on email delivery.
key_points: Email delivery of registration keys is unreliable, especially to institutional addresses with aggressive spam filters
A customer purchasing twice likely signals a delivery failure, not enthusiasm — and ignoring it risks chargebacks
When a customer accidentally double-purchases, proactively crediting them builds loyalty worth far more than the refund amount
Three similar incidents constitute a pattern requiring a systemic fix, not individual support responses
Displaying registration keys inline on the website eliminates dependency on email delivery
money_quotes: 505 — 'This message will be archived and *may* be presented to the recipient', surely one of the least informative error messages of all time.
Every additional copy of my software sold is free money.

A customer who has already demonstrated their willingness to purchase *two* copies from you, on different days no less, has a huge likelihood to purchase a third, which will pay for that $6 discount 3 times over.
The jawdroppingly excellent customer service you've just provided them makes them feel all warm and fuzzy inside and likely to drive a fourth, fifth, seventy-second purchase along your way.
I want to rely less on the automated email reaching my customers, which means displaying their registration key inline in my website.
This is going to actually take some web programming, which I hate with a burning passion (Perl still gives me hives).
key_insight: When the same customer problem recurs three times, stop treating it as individual support incidents and fix the system — the cost of a small engineering improvement is far less than the compounding cost of lost customers and chargebacks.
